chunk_summary: Guidelines for antithyroid therapy during lactation emphasize methimazole or propylthiouracil at moderate doses, with careful monitoring of infant thyroid function and maternal health. Timing doses post-breastfeeding minimizes infant exposure.
learning_objectives: Understand the factors influencing medication transfer into breast milk.
Learn safe dosing guidelines for methimazole and propylthiouracil during lactation.
Recognize the importance of monitoring infant thyroid function and maternal health during antithyroid therapy.
Identify incompatible therapies like radioiodine and plan alternatives.
key_facts: Methimazole is preferred over propylthiouracil due to lower maternal hepatotoxicity risk.
Compatible dosing: Methimazole up to 20–30 mg/day; Propylthiouracil up to 300–450 mg/day.
Doses should be taken immediately after breastfeeding to reduce infant exposure.
Routine infant thyroid testing is not required unless symptoms arise.
Radioiodine therapy is incompatible with breastfeeding and requires specialist planning.
reference_quote: Current evidence supports both methimazole and propylthiouracil during lactation at moderate doses, with careful maternal dosing and clinical monitoring of the infant.
